dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond to an unknown problem or person down at Richmond.
Morning, I'm EMT Alex. Can you tell me why 911 was called today?
The patient was unresponsive in a parked vehicle; found with a GCS of 7 initially.
On arrival, he was opening his eyes to pain, left side slightly more than right.
Gave voice commands but no verbal response early, just grunting.
Drawer test elicited flexion of all four extremities, GCS improved with movement.
Airway patent, no signs or sounds of obstruction, SCOA was successful.
Breathing is regular and non-labored, clear bilateral breath sounds.
Radial pulses are equal and strong. Skintone warm and dry.
He became more alert; self-reported he had "too much to drink" after working overnight.
Denies SI or HI, reports he takes Metformin and Pioglitazone for type 2 diabetes.
Last meal or drink was at 7 am; reports nausea, denies emesis.
Nausea was his only symptom, no pain reported.
He's denied any pain, just stated he "didn't feel well" at first.
Skin is warm, dry, and intact. No abrasions or visible lesions.
Pupils are equal and reactive with no extraneous eye movement observed.
No nuchal rigidity or swallowing issues noted at this time.
No drainage from orifices; he had slurred speech when more alert.
Chest appears clear with no deformity or abnormal sounds.
Normal abdominal sounds noted to upper quadrants, no rigidity or rebound tenderness.
Gross motor function is symmetric. No abnormal angulation or instability.
Initial pulse is 70; later readings 71, 82, and 83 with no rhythm irregularities.
Respiration rate is noted at 16 and 14, non-labored, even throughout.
First BP was 0/0; later climbed to 152/92, then dropped to 115/72, and finally 97/66.
Glucose was reported to be 0 at first, then back to 0, with a high of 338 before transport.
SpO2 started low: 0 and 97, but improved to 100 and 99 during transport.
4 lead monitoring started as per standing order with first stable reading as sinus rhythm.
First IV attempt at 18G in left AC was unsuccessful; 20G IV started in patient's left hand.
12-lead ECG obtained with nonspecific intraventricular conduction delay and ST elevation noted.
350 mL of normal saline administered per protocol with stable trending vital signs observed.
Second BP was 115/72 and then dropped again to 97/66 with consistent sinus rhythm noted.
No further meds or incompatibilities noted, IV solution was well-tolerated and effective.
Secured for transport in semi-Fowler's position with all available restraints applied.
Hyperglycemic protocol 3-7 was followed from IV to ECG findings and normal saline administration.
Moved from car to stretcher with EMS lifting under arms and slid into ER bed successfully.
Directed to VCU Health Systems Cold for ER arrival and care handoff as planned.
He's alert, oriented, and in sinus rhythm. Metformin and Pioglitazone on board for your review.
Patient reported intoxication after working overnight, no documented allergies, and no injuries found.
Initial GCS 7, improved to 13 and remained alert. ECG trended from elevated ST segments to borderline.
Transported using EMS stretcher into ER; transition to hospital running smoothly.
Final respiration count is 14 per minute, normal work of breathing.
All airway devices remain clear with no new interventions needed while in ER.
Final self-report is coherent, denial of pain, and no new symptoms reported.
Final pulse ox noted at 99% with no change; stable via supplemental oxygen in ER area.
Delivered safely to ER with initial glucose spike and final trending back to normal levels.
Report drafted using submitted protocols. All notes entered into VTS system. Transfer complete.
ECG showed epicardial injury suspicion and early repolarization was noted on interpretation.
No clinical signs of pericarditis were found during transport; ST changes remained per reported interpretation.
